author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

title: Chapter 59

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 59.—But according to all these four hypotheses, the truth is on the side of the communion of the Catholic Church.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if both are true, then you certainly should not have deserted the communion of the whole world on account of men such as you too had among yourselves.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if both are false, you should have guarded against the guilt of most accursed division, which had not even any pretext to allege of any delivery of the sacred books.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If ours are true and yours are false, you have long been without anything to say for yourselves.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If yours are true and ours are false, we have been liable to be deceived, in common with the whole world, not about the truth of the faith, but about the unrighteousness of men.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For the seed of Abraham, dispersed throughout the world, was bound to pay attention, not to what you said you knew, but to what you proved to the judges.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Whence have we any knowledge of what was done by those men who were accused by your ancestors, even if the allegations made against them were true, so long as they were held to be not true but false, either by the judges who took cognizance of the case, or at least by the general body of the Church dispersed throughout the world, which was only bound to pay heed to the sentence of the judges?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: God does not necessarily pardon any human guilt that others in the weakness of human judgment fail to discover; yet I maintain that no one is rightly deemed guilty for having believed a man to be innocent who was not convicted.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: How then do you prove the world to be guilty, merely because it did not know what possibly was really guilt in the Africans,—its ignorance arising either from the fact that no one reported the sin to it, or from its having given credence, in respect of the information which was given, rather to the judges who took cognizance of the case, than to the murmurers who were defeated?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: So far then, Petilianus deserves all praise, in that, when he saw that on this point I was absolutely impregnable, he passed it by in silence.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Yet he does not deserve praise for his attempts to obscure in a mist of words other points which were equally impregnable, which yet he thought could be obscured; or for having put me in the place of his cause, when the cause left him nothing to say; while even about myself he could say nothing except what was either altogether false, or undeserving of any blame, or without any bearing whatsoever upon me.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But, in the meantime, are you, whom I have made judges between Petilianus and myself, possessed of discrimination enough to decide in any degree between what is true and what is false, between what is mere empty swelling and what is solid, between what is troubled and what is calm, between inflammation and soundness, between divine predictions and human assumptions, between bringing an accusation and establishing it, between proofs and fictions, between pleading a cause and leading one away from it?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If you have such power of discrimination, well and good; but if you have it not, we shall not repent of having bestowed our pains on you, for even though your heart be not converted unto peace, yet our peace shall return unto ourselves.
